commonIssues: Powdery mildew on foliage - water at soil level, not on leaves, and give plants air circulation
Slugs and snails damage young plants
Occasional leaf spot and rust when leaves stay wet
Aphids and Japanese beetles occasionally feed on plants
Struggles in poorly drained, constantly wet soil - ensure container drainage
Short-lived perennial or biennial - often regrown each year; it self-seeds prolifically unless deadheaded
Tall cultivars (over 60 cm) in windy spots may flop and benefit from a discreet stake, though support is normally unnecessary

source.citation: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Rudbeckia hirta: full sun; average, medium-moisture, well-drained soil; drought tolerant; struggles in wet, poorly drained soil; USDA Zones 3-7; blooms June-September; powdery mildew and slugs/snails noted; low maintenance; biennial/short-lived perennial that blooms first year from seed
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Rudbeckia hirta: Zone 3 to 7 hardiness; bloom June to September; deadheading prolongs bloom and limits self-seeding

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - Rudbeckia factsheet: full sun (greater than 6 hours of direct sunlight), tolerates partial shade; drought and heat tolerant once established; slow-release 12-6-6 fertilizer in April when growth emerges and again at half rate in September; dwarf cultivars suit containers; powdery mildew, leaf spot, rust with wet foliage; aphids, Japanese beetles, spider mites occasional
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - Rudbeckia factsheet: full sun defined as more than 6 hours direct sun per day; adapts to partial shade

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Rudbeckia hirta: USDA Zones 3a-8b; full sun (6+ hours) or partial shade (2-6 hours); moist, well-drained soil; tolerates occasionally dry conditions, moderately drought-resistant; 2-4 ft tall, 12 in spacing; blooms late spring into mid-fall with deadheading; powdery mildew, slugs, snails
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Rudbeckia hirta: Zones 3a-8b hardiness; biennial or short-lived perennial blooming first year from seed; bloom continues into mid-fall with deadheading

RHS - Rudbeckia hirta (plant 68850): hardiness H3 (-5 to 1 C) when grown as a half-hardy annual; full sun or partial shade; moist but well-drained soil; 0.5-1 m tall, 0.1-0.5 m spread; biennial or short-lived perennial often grown as an annual; flowers summer to autumn
RHS - Rudbeckia hirta: moist but well-drained conditions preferred; tolerates clay and loam

Confidence note: no authoritative source states an exact watering interval in days; baseIntervalDays=3 is a conservative container-growing estimate derived from 'medium moisture, water when soil begins to dry, drought tolerant once established' guidance (Missouri Botanical Garden, NC State). Adjust by soil-dryness check.

Confidence note: container size is not specified by the cited extension sources beyond 'dwarf cultivars are ideal for containers' (Clemson); recommendedMinLiters=11 (approx. 3 gal / 30 cm pot) is an estimate sized to the species' 2-4 ft height and fibrous, relatively shallow root system. Larger pots improve stability and bloom.

Confidence note: feeding interval estimated. Clemson recommends slow-release 12-6-6 twice per season (April and September); baseIntervalDays=90 approximates that schedule. The species needs only moderate fertility - excess nitrogen reduces flowering.

Confidence note: heatStressC=35 is an estimate; sources describe the plant as heat tolerant (Clemson) but give no numeric threshold. coldHardyMinC=-34 reflects USDA Zone 4 as a conservative reading of the Zone 3-7/3a-8b ratings (MoBot, NC State); plants grown as half-hardy annuals from spring transplants should still be hardened off (RHS H3).
